Scenario.title: Pikabot Attack Scenario on Water Infrastructure
Scenario.overview: Water and Wastewater Systems Complex This narrative depicts a sophisticated multi-stage cyber attack attributed to the Pikabot malware group, leveraging expert-level tactics to compromise operational technology environments in February 2024. The attack integrates reconnaissance, persistence, lateral movement, and impact phases to disrupt critical water treatment processes.

threat_actor_profile.motivation: Espionage and potential sabotage aimed at gaining unauthorized access to control systems.

Scenario.attack_surface: Open Source Intelligence (OSINT) Internet-facing assets Public Media Windows Registry Domain Name Registrar Target System Artifact File Process OS Process
Scenario.attack_vector: Artificial Intelligence Network Connection Creation (Mechanism) General Access Method Image File Execution Options Injection Windows Registry Key Creation (Mechanism) Remote Desktop Protocol Process Creation (Mechanism) Manipulation of Control

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: An attacker begins by gathering extensive information about the water treatment facility through publicly available sources, including employee directories, vendor details, and network architecture data scraped online. This phase identifies potential vulnerabilities and entry points, guiding the customization of the Pikabot malware to exploit specific OT protocols like SCADA or Modbus commonly found in such infrastructures.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.description: In this stage, the threat actor designs a tailored version of the Pikabot malware by injecting code into templates or network flows. Using insights from reconnaissance, they incorporate evasion techniques to bypass standard security measures, focusing on adapting the malware to interact seamlessly with industrial control systems prevalent in water management, thereby increasing its effectiveness in compromising OT environments.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: The attack gains initial foothold through a spearphishing campaign, sending highly targeted emails to authorized personnel disguised as legitimate communications. Embedded hyperlinks lead users to download malicious ZIP archives, prompting interaction that allows the malware to infect endpoints. This leverages human psychology and existing trust relationships to breach defenses focused on external threats.

stage.name: Execution
stage.technique_name: T1183: Image File Execution Options Injection
stage.description: Upon user interaction with the spearphished link, the malicious archive executes embedded scripts or binaries via image file injection techniques. This exploits vulnerabilities in how systems handle binary files, allowing the malware to run silently and establish a command-and-control connection, thus initiating further malicious activities within the network.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: To maintain long-term access, the malware configures scheduled tasks or modifies Windows registry keys, ensuring it runs automatically after system reboots or during idle times. This persistence mechanism prevents easy removal and allows continuous operation, blending with legitimate system processes to sustain the attack lifecycle undetected.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.capec: CAPEC-67: String Format Overflow in syslog()
stage.description: The attacker elevates privileges by exploiting vulnerabilities in system artifacts, such as default passwords or password reuse patterns identified during reconnaissance. Techniques like TEE (Trusted Execution Environment) exploits allow unauthorized access to administrative controls over OT components, granting broader capabilities to manipulate critical infrastructure settings.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: Malware employs obfuscation and encryption to alter its code dynamically, hiding activities from signature-based detection tools. By abusing elevation control mechanisms, it avoids triggering alerts in endpoint protection software, maintaining stealth throughout the operational technology network to evade monitoring and analysis.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC-461: Web Services API Signature Forgery Leveraging Hash Function Extension Weakness
stage.description: Compromised systems enable harvesting of hashed credentials stored in memory or databases, facilitated by tools like Mimikatz. This allows the attacker to move laterally within the network, stealing authentication tokens to access protected resources without needing plaintext passwords, thereby expanding their footprint across various water system components.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: After gaining credentials, the malware scans internal networks to discover connected devices, particularly SCADA controllers or PLCs, by examining file configurations and network mappings. This helps identify communication channels and authenticate to devices, probing for exploitable services to bridge between trusted and untrusted zones for deeper penetration.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.description: Leveraging stolen credentials obtained earlier, the attacker moves laterally by establishing Remote Desktop Protocol connections to isolated segments of the water system's OT environment. This spreads the infection across multiple nodes, escalating privileges and accessing restricted areas to maximize control over the infrastructure.

stage.name: Collection
stage.description: Malicious actors collect sensitive operational data, such as chemical dosing schedules, equipment statuses, and process control variables, by querying and exfiltrating information from targeted system artifacts. This data theft occurs post-infection, aiming to understand the facility's operations fully before planning disruptive actions.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.description: Collected data is exfiltrated covertly by encoding it into DNS queries and transmitting it over seemingly normal network traffic. This method uses process creation mechanisms to spawn hidden threads, masking the exfiltration from standard monitoring tools and ensuring it blends with routine internet activity without raising suspicion.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC-166: Force the System to Reset Values
stage.description: The final stage involves manipulating control processes by altering settings on pumps, valves, or sensors within the water treatment facility. This causes intentional operational disruptions, such as incorrect chemical dosing or halted processes, potentially leading to service failures or safety incidents, demonstrating the capability to influence real-world outcomes through digital intrusion.

Scenario.summary: The Pikabot campaign successfully infiltrated a water and wastewater system through expertly crafted spearphishing, established persistent malware presence, and escalated privileges to manipulate OT controls, causing significant operational risks.
